verses.commentary: The angels arrive at evening. Lot sits in the gateway, suggesting he has become a civic leader. He shows hospitality like Abraham, bowing low. He recognizes the need to welcome strangers.
Lot invites them urgently. The square is the public area, unsafe. The angels initially refuse, perhaps testing Lot's insistence or revealing Sodom's danger.
He insisted strongly. Lot knows the danger. Bread without yeast (unleavened) is quick to make, suggesting urgency. They enter his house.
Before bed, crisis erupts. All the men from every part emphasizes totality. Both young and old means complete participation. The entire city is corrupt.
Gang rape is demanded. This is the depth of Sodom's wickedness: violent sexual assault, abuse of strangers, complete breakdown of hospitality and morality.
Lot exits and shuts the door, protecting the guests. He confronts the mob alone.
My friends (literally my brothers) is conciliatory. Don't do this wicked thing. Lot recognizes the evil clearly.

Lot's shocking offer. Two virgin daughters instead of the guests. You can do what you like is horrifying. This is not righteousness but desperation. The protection of my roof invokes sacred hospitality. Lot values hospitality over his daughters' safety, revealing both cultural values and moral compromise.

The mob rejects Lot. You came as a foreigner. Now he wants to judge. They resent an outsider's moral stand. We'll treat you worse is a threat of violence. They attack the door. The situation escalates.
The angels intervene. They pull Lot to safety. Divine protection.
Supernatural blindness. Young and old again emphasizes totality. They fumble for the door, unable to find it. Judgment begins.
Urgent escape plan. Anyone else means family, extended relatives. Get them out. Mercy offers a way of escape.
We are going to destroy. Divine judgment is certain. The outcry (18:20-21) has been confirmed. God sent us. The angels are agents of destruction.

Lot warns his sons-in-law. Pledged to marry suggests betrothal. Hurry, urgent command. But they thought he was joking. They don't believe him. Lot's credibility is compromised by his compromised life in Sodom.
Dawn breaks. The angels urge, literally press him. Hurry! Only four people: Lot, his wife, two daughters. Swept away means destroyed. When the city is punished, literally in the iniquity of the city.

He hesitated. Lot lingers, reluctant to leave. The angels grasp their hands forcibly. Led them safely out. For the LORD was merciful. This is grace. Lot doesn't deserve rescue, but God remembers Abraham (verse 29).
Three commands. Flee for your lives. Don't look back. Don't stop. Flee to the mountains. Complete separation is required.
Even in crisis, Lot negotiates. No suggests resistance. He doesn't want the mountains.
Lot acknowledges mercy. But fears the mountains. I'll die. Lack of faith.
Lot proposes Zoar. Small (mits'ar) sounds like Zoar. He repeats small/little to emphasize insignificance. Let me go there.
The angel concedes. Very well. I will grant this request. Zoar is spared for Lot's sake.
Urgency again. I cannot do anything until you reach it. God waits for Lot's safety before judging. Parenthetical note: the town was called Zoar commemorates this event.
Full daylight. The timing is precise. Lot arrives safely.
Judgment falls. The LORD rained from the LORD. Trinitarian hint or emphatic construction. Burning sulfur, brimstone and fire. Complete destruction from heaven.
Overthrew means turned upside down. Total devastation. Cities, plain, people, vegetation. Nothing survives.
Lot's wife looks back, violating the command (verse 17). She becomes a pillar of salt. Judgment for disobedience and perhaps longing for Sodom. Jesus refers to this (Luke 17:32). Don't look back.
Abraham rises early, returns to the place of intercession. He wants to see what happened.
Abraham sees the smoke. Dense smoke like a furnace. The cities are gone. The intercession didn't save Sodom because there weren't even ten righteous.
God remembered Abraham. Lot is saved not for his own merit but because of Abraham. This explains the rescue. Brought Lot out. Divine mercy extends to Abraham's family.
Lot leaves Zoar. Ironically, he ends up in the mountains he initially feared. He lives in a cave. Regression from city to cave. His choices led to isolation.
Daughters' plot. Our father is old. No man around. They see themselves as the last people. The custom means way. They want offspring.
The plan: intoxicate father, commit incest, preserve the line. This is morally wrong but reveals desperation. Lot's compromises bear bitter fruit.
They execute the plan. Lot is drunk, unaware. The text does not condone this but reports it.
The plan repeats. Both daughters. Preserve our family line justifies the act in their minds.
Second night. Same pattern. Lot is unconscious both times.
Both conceive. The result is two sons.
Moab may mean from father. The Moabites are Israel's neighbors, often hostile. Ruth was a Moabite, showing God's grace extends to all.
Ben-Ammi means son of my people. The Ammonites are also Israel's neighbors and often enemies. Both nations originate from incest, yet God will later show mercy to individuals from these peoples.
